title: The Mind's Eye
author: Oliver Sacks
excerpt: Oliver Sacks turns his attention to how we perceive the visual world—exploring cases of people who lose the ability to read, recognize faces, or see in three dimensions, and how the brain's extraordinary plasticity allows them to adapt and find new ways of seeing.
keyIdeas: Visual perception is an active, constructive process, not passive reception
The brain can adapt remarkably when one sensory system is damaged
Visual imagery and visual perception use overlapping neural circuits
Face blindness (prosopagnosia) reveals how specialized facial recognition is
keyTakeaways: The brain's plasticity allows remarkable adaptations to sensory loss
Our experience of the visual world is a construction, not a recording
Neurological conditions reveal the normal functioning of the brain
There are many ways to 'see'—vision is not the only path to visual understanding
whoShouldRead: Fans of Oliver Sacks's neurological case studies
Anyone interested in visual perception and the brain
Artists and designers who work with visual media
People experiencing or caring for someone with visual impairment
whoShouldSkip: Readers looking for practical advice or systematic frameworks
Those who prefer structured argument to clinical narratives
